articles.summary: The post Former FDA Officials: There’s Opportunity To Rebuild The Agency — But Not The Way It Was appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Lawyers Should Sometimes Talk Less
articles.content:
律师以健谈著称。由于律师的职责是为客户辩护，有些律师可能认为必须多说才能有效完成工作。此外，有些律师天生就健谈，这可能是他们进入这个职业的原因之一。然而，有时候律师最好的选择是保持沉默，而不是为客户进行辩护。

在我职业生涯早期，我曾为一个客户进行案件辩论，法院安排了一场关于临时限制令的电话会议，只提前约一小时通知我。对手遇到了技术问题。我跃跃欲试，问法官是否应该开始陈述我客户的案件，因为我没有遇到技术困难。法官随后正确地提醒我，由于对手是申请方，他们应该首先就该事项发表意见。

在通话过程中，法官对对手的立场表现出强烈的敌意。对手需要克服重大障碍才能获得其所寻求的救济，而他根本没有充分证明自己的案件。虽然我非常想为客户进行陈述，但我可以看出，即使我一个字都不说，对手也会输，而如果我说了什么，可能会让事情复杂化。最终，我代表客户赢得了这个案件，在整个听证会上我总共只说了几句话。

在我职业生涯的另一个时期，我有一个案件的法庭会议，所有各方都非常接近达成和解。因此，每个人都希望推迟会议，以便有更多时间讨论和解事宜。我问对手是否想在客户之前向法院请求延期，他说更愿意在预定会议时直接请求延期。

当法院书记员宣读所有案件时，各方可以请求延期或进行会议讨论。对手请求进行会议讨论，随后解释说，他是想向法院通报和解谈判的情况，而不是仅仅请求延期。我对这个做法不满意，因为我们需要在法庭上再坐90分钟来与法院开会讨论，而不是仅仅请求延期然后立即离开。

当我们终于被叫到进行会议讨论时，对手全程都在讲述案件的情况、主张以及和解谈判的进展。法院书记员问我们是否需要延期，我们得以将案件延期一段时间，以便最终确定和解方案。我不明白为什么对手不能在出庭一开始就请求延期然后离开。也许他想向客户收取更多费用，或者只是喜欢听自己的声音。然而，这让我们浪费了近两个小时，本来可以通过少说话来节省这些时间。

总而言之，律师在听证会、法庭会议等场合可能会感到一种自然的冲动想说更多，因为他们可能认为多说对客户有益。然而，在某些情况下，律师少说话往往能更好地为客户服务。

乔丹·罗斯曼是罗斯曼律师事务所的合伙人，这是一家提供全方位服务的纽约和新泽西律师事务所。他还是“学生贷款日记”网站的创始人，该网站讨论他是如何还清学生贷款的。你可以通过电子邮件jordan@rothman.law联系乔丹。

本文首次发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Lawyers are known to be talkative. Since attorneys are paid to advocate for their clients, some lawyers may feel as if they have to speak more in order to effectively complete their jobs. Moreover, some lawyers are just talkative people, and this might be one of the reasons that they entered the profession. However, sometimes lawyers are best served by keeping quiet rather than advocating on behalf of a client.

Earlier in my career, I had to argue a matter for a client, and the court scheduled a telephone conference for a temporary restraining order on about an hour’s notice. My adversary was having technical issues. I was raring to go and asked the court if I should start presenting my client’s case since I was not having technical difficulties. The court then correctly advised me that since my adversary was the movant, they should have the first say on the matter.

During the call, the judge was extremely hostile to my adversary’s position. My adversary had significant hurdles to clear in order to obtain the relief he sought, and he simply did not make out a sufficient case to obtain such relief. Although I really wanted to make the case for my client, I could tell that my adversary was going to lose without me even saying a word, and if I said something, I might complicate matters. In the end, I won the matter on behalf of my client, and I only said a few words in total at the hearing.

At another time in my career, I had a court conference on a case in which all parties were pretty close to a settlement. Accordingly, everyone wanted an adjournment of the conference to have more time to talk about the settlement. I asked my adversary if he wanted to ask the court for an adjournment in advance of the client, and he said that he preferred to ask for the adjournment at the scheduled conference itself.

When the court attorney called all of the cases on the docket, people could either ask for an adjournment or a conference. My adversary asked for a conference and later explained that he wanted to advise the court of the settlement talks rather than merely ask for an adjournment. I was not happy with this approach, since we needed to sit in the courtroom for another 90 minutes to conference the matter with the court rather than merely ask for an adjournment and leave immediately.

When we were finally called to conference the case, my adversary spoke the entire time about what the case was about, the claims, and the status of settlement talks. The court attorney asked if we wanted an adjournment, and we were able to adjourn the matter for a while so that we could finalize the settlement. I have no idea why my adversary could not just ask for an adjournment and leave earlier in the appearance. Perhaps this adversary wanted to bill more time to his client, or perhaps he just loved to hear the sound of his own voice.  However, this cost us nearly two hours that could have been saved by speaking less.

All told, lawyers might feel a natural urge to talk more at hearings, court conferences, and the like since they might think that more speaking benefits clients. However, lawyers would often be better served by speaking less in certain circumstances.

Jordan Rothman is a partner of The Rothman Law Firm, a full-service New York and New Jersey law firm. He is also the founder of Student Debt Diaries, a website discussing how he paid off his student loans. You can reach Jordan through email at jordan@rothman.law.

The post Lawyers Should Sometimes Talk Less appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: $225K Law School Theft Scheme Costs Attorney His License
articles.content:
我们曾报道过一些很酷的法学院副业——作为《危险边缘！》参赛者赚取六位数收入是个人的最爱。但这并不是学生赚取六位数的唯一方式。一位律师在法学院期间找到了一种方法，窃取了数十万美元。这只是让他失去了执照。俄亥俄州法院新闻对此进行了报道：

俄亥俄州最高法院今天永久取消了一名加利亚县律师的执业资格，该律师因从其管理的遗产中窃取超过22.5万美元而被定罪。

内森·哈维在担任律师助理并就读法学院期间，律师布里特·怀斯曼安排哈维担任一处遗产的管理人。在开立遗产银行账户后不久，哈维立即开始窃取资金。怀斯曼后来发现了盗窃行为，并向纪律部门报告了哈维的行为。

俄亥俄州最高法院首席法官沙龙·L·肯尼迪在多数意见中写道，哈维的案件是俄亥俄州首例，“这是一个令人不安的首例：一名法学院学生在两年零八个月的时间里实施盗窃计划，挪用客户资金，直到他获得法律实习生认证并获准执业后才被抓获。”

取消执业资格是一项纠正措施。哈维之前被判处60个月的社区监督，并被无限期停职，但他对客户信任的严重滥用需要更严厉的处罚。你不常听到有人在法律职业生涯开始之前就毁掉了它。有趣的是：法学院毕业后，哈维继续成为了一名助理检察官。如果我知道自己从某人那里偷了六位数，我会尽最大努力远离那个办公室。这表明，像律师一样思考并不需要常识。

对于每一个怀着明确赚钱目的去读法学院的人来说，这可不是正确的方式。

俄亥俄州法院永久取消窃取客户遗产22.5万美元律师的执业资格[俄亥俄州法院新闻]
articles.summary:
We’ve covered some very cool law school side hustles — raking in six figures as a “Jeopardy!” contestant is a personal favorite. That isn’t the only way to rake in six figures as a student though. An attorney found a way to skim hundreds of thousands of dollars while he was still a law student. It just cost him his license. Court News Ohio has coverage:

The Supreme Court of Ohio today permanently disbarred a Gallia County attorney who was convicted of theft after stealing more than $225,000 from an estate he administered.

Nathan Harvey was a legal assistant attending law school when attorney Britt Wiseman arranged for Harvey to serve as the administrator of an estate. Shortly after opening a bank account for the estate, Harvey immediately began stealing from it. Wiseman later discovered the theft and reported Harvey’s actions to disciplinary authorities.
…
Writing for the Supreme Court majority, Chief Justice Sharon L. Kennedy stated that Harvey’s case is a first for Ohio, “and a disturbing case of first impression it is: a law student who put into action a theft scheme to misappropriate client funds for two years and eight months and did not get caught until” after he was certified as a legal intern and admitted to practice law.

The disbarment was a corrective measure. Harvey was previously sentenced to 60 months of community control and had his license indefinitely suspended, but the severity of his abuse of client trust deserved a harsher sentence. You don’t hear about someone ruining their legal career before they start it every day. Fun fact: after law school, Harvey went on to become an assistant prosecutor. Call me crazy, but if I knew I stole six figures from someone, I’d be doing my damndest to stay as far away from that office as possible. Goes to show that thinking like a lawyer doesn’t require common sense.

For every hopeful going to law school with the express purpose of earning money, this is not the way to do it.

Court Disbars Attorney Who Stole $225,000 From Client’s Estate [Court News Ohio]

Chris Williams became a social media manager and assistant editor for Above the Law in June 2021. Prior to joining the staff, he moonlighted as a minor Memelord™ in the Facebook group Law School Memes for Edgy T14s .  He endured Missouri long enough to graduate from Washington University in St. Louis School of Law. He is a former boat builder who is learning to swim and is interested in rhetoric, Spinozists and humor. Getting back in to cycling wouldn’t hurt either. You can reach him by email at christopherrashadwilliams@gmail.com and by Tweet/Bluesky at @WritesForRent.

The post $225K Law School Theft Scheme Costs Attorney His License appeared first on Above the Law.
